speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Coyote.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was a coyote?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Yeah, it was a coyote. And he went up on the hill and he made a turn then, and he got about halfway up the hill, and he came right down the old stand right where I was. He went up on that Mount Holm Bridge up here, that's just about where I was standing, one of the two big trees there. And he came from that brush up there, right down through there, and I don't think it is from that door there. When he called through the fence across the road, I let him have it, and I got him right there. He rolled over right in the road, and pulled up coming down from Garfield, he was slaying, and he looked at that coyote a little bit.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: He looked at that coyote a little bit and he said, did you shoot him?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Yeah, I said, you would have laughed if you had been here a little while ago. I said, you know he went right through there, there were men scattered here, and he kept looking this way, and he went right up through that bunch of women, and they couldn't nobody shoot because they were shooting right in, dead.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I said, they were about 50 feet apart when he went through, they just kept opening up, and hollering the yellow open, about 100 people down there. And they finally came over, the whole bunch of them came up there, and they came up there and wanted to see that coyote. I said, there he is, I said, right there, right down the road.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: And I walked over and picked him up, and the guy said, where I was standing, where is he, just where I was standing, right there, where did he come through, right up there?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: You hear him as he comes through, yes he comes through the fence, and yes he got through the fence, I let him have it. And he jumped out into the road and had it all, but there was snow on too, snow on. They thought that was an awful shot.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I seen one time they were coming down the hill like that, and the dog was coming behind, and he came down like this, and he came down here, and he must smell me, he started up the hill, and I threw the rifle up like that, and I banged and knocked him down right there. He was jumping along too, and boy hit him right through the river, boy he went down to the bank. I went to come up there and on top of him, and they pulled him down into the road, and I went over and got him, and put him down there by the house, and they all started snuffing around. My two dogs, they knew me, they came right to me, and I put the chains on, tied them up right there. I waited for somebody else to start. And by golly, I know what was going on, and now from that time, here come another one down the same old road. And he done the same damn thing to this other one, he just jumped right down, he must have smelled me, the wind was blowing, he smelled me, he turned up the hill, and I got him down.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I was all quipped, and I think, he came walking down, he came down there where I was, and he said, did you get them both hearts?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Yes, I got them both. I told the boys, I said, I met him up there, I bet I just down there in his old stand, he'd all killed one or two there, he killed two today, he got them both, he said we were down there, he got them both, and I had them hanging up there in the old barn,

speaker: Archie Clark
words: and I said, where's Buster? Anybody know where he's at?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: No, they said we don't know.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Buster said, that spotted dog?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Yes. And then I heard he was up on the hill, back at Sweet John's place. Boy, I said, he's up on the hill. Oh, Sweet John had a place that I thought he had, he was living on the, cutting the piney, and he homesteaded it. When he died a while, they didn't pay no attention, they just went back to the government, they just took it just like it was, they never changed it. And he got seeded, boy, he cut a lot of stuff out of that old 160 up there, right in the timber. Boy, he had a field up there about eight acres, he'd seed that, he'd plow that out in the fall and then he'd seed it down to Oats in the spring, and he'd cut it for hay for his horses. That's the way he lived. And he got hurt one time, and he'd all avoided all the pensions for being hurt. And he still joined, he joined up till he died. And the county had to, the county had to pay a brilliant, pretty deep snow. That's taking it out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you take that?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: No, Sherry's friend took it, another friend took it. Yeah, he bought that and then another one there. And I, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see how he tattered it up my knees?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I'm right in the same place, just about 30 feet from me here in front of me. There was an opening there, I went back to the barn, and up and the top broke off of that tree, it just broke off. And it left about 20 feet of, oh, that's bigger than it was. But the wind hit awful hard in there, and it was a big bushy tree, and it bloated it down. It broke it right off. And I was standing there back in some brush right here, and I heard, oh, Bill, as I called him, Bill, that's my dog. And he'd cut the whole damn work there if you run across the link, or cat, bobcat, and he'd be working on it too. He'd be fetching them.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: He's up above old Sweet John's place, and he'd come right down the ridge, right down the long days of the brush, come right down like this in here. I said, you son of a gun, if he fetches that, he must be after a cat or bobcat, a link of bobcat. And I said, by golly, I know, he brought that right down, and he crossed the road right in 30 feet of me, and went up there, and he turned around and looked over this way, and he looked that way, and the wind was blowing, and the new bus just came out in the opening. Boy, he was coming. And he turned around and went up, and set right up in the top of that tree, right there in 30 feet of me. And then he said, and old Buster kept tearing up, he seen me, and he stopped, and he looked up, and boy, he went to barking. Oh boy, he was just pulling that old tree, and barking, and barking, and barking. And I said, well Buster, I'll shoot him for you. And I up, and I knocked him out of the tree, and he came down just to go punk in that snow. Old Buster was right on top of me. He dragged him out, and he dragged him about halfway over to me, hanging on to him, and I finally made him let loose. And I had him laying on the log right there, and old Buster was sitting there sitting beside him, I put the chain on him so he couldn't get away, and the hounds were still running, they were still going around, and around, and around, and finally they did hit this trail, and they come right back down, and I don't know what in the world, I guess they can smell me. Anyway, Buster comes down to me about 30 minutes at that table there, when he's turning him up the hill, just like the rest of them, and I'll throw the gun up like that, and bang, down he went. And there was five hounds after him, my one dog, and I think he had two, and I had two. And another neighbor up there, he had a dog, he'd always get it with him. I don't know if he was much of a dog, but he barked, that's all. He never touched the cow, he never got near it. He just stopped right in the road and looked at the rest of them fighting, and there's a fighting, and three dogs were just going after him. Boy, I had old Buster, I wouldn't let him get. And old Buster, he'd be running around the whole bunch, and he was a fast dog, and he was fast. I had two dogs of mine, and Dick and Link, and he had one named Dick. I'm like, boy, now I tell you, when I outrun them three, they had to be going. They had to be head started when they started to land there, and they were the damndest dogs I ever seen. They was running in that timber and up in that so much that they'd stop and let them, and then they'd take it across, and the hounds would go up here, and he'd get away, and hell, they'd hit that trail way up there. He had to, quarter of a mile, he had the rest of the hounds. Man would they lace it to it then. Man, the three of them. Sometimes I have old Buster be with me, and I tie him up. And if he run across, it'll make a difference how many dogs there was in that bunch. We've had 30 dogs in that bunch, and then he'd quit. And the first guy, I hear him barking. I said, old Buster's got a bobcat on Link, something, he's fetching it around, either he, and the other dogs would go on up towards the gentleman's place and back around over the hill, back in there, back at Eric's place, and around, and make a circle way up in there. If somebody didn't shoot him before he come back, I'd always get him, right there in that same place. And, uh, old Buster quit.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I said, Buster's quit to myself. I got away, and then he's after now. No, no. The first guy I heard him barking, I knew what he had. I just knew he was barking. Man, he was painting that old cat around, and around, and around up there. By golly, the first thing the old cat started down, and that daggone cat stayed right in the road. He hit the road up there, and he never left. He'd come right down to me. Waited about 20 feet from me when I shot him with a rifle. And, damn, he was coming. I stepped out like that, and I had the gun on, I stepped out, and he looked at me, and I kind of braced himself to stop, and I let him have it.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: And I told him over the log, and he come down, and he said, What in the time there was Buster at?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: And he looked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you shoot him?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Yes, I shot him right there in the road. By golly, I said, I told the boys that old Buster was after something. I said, I shot him about 20 dogs after this one, by golly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made Buster such a good dog? How did he get to be such a good dog?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Well, I only had the two of them. I had him alone first, and I was running cats out. I'd take the old dog cat up there and turn him loose. He'd start from home, and then after he'd run, Buster would run about, and coil him wild, and then he'd take a tree. And I'd go down there and pet old Buster, and stop him, and he couldn't get up there. He couldn't dwell. And the old cat would be up there looking down at me, and finally I'd call the old cat down. I could catch the old cat any place, and call him down. I'd pet him, and oh, Buster's coming around to smell the old cat, and he'd say, I could just see him look at me and say, Oh, shit. That's that old dog cat. Ha, ha, ha, ha, ha, ha, ha, ha, ha, ha.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Oh, boy, they would come down and say, Did you get him, Martin?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Yeah, I got him. He just lived quite a mile from us. I think Martin did.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: He'd come down, he'd come over to that place, and I said, You see that coyote up there on the hill?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: My gosh, yes.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: He said, I tell him, How in the devil are you going to get to him?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: He said, I haven't got you, though. I said, I tell you what you do. You take Buster and go around.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I said, The old dog will follow you. He'll follow you. You just need Buster. He went around the brush, around the fence, and then way around, and then come back up on the hill. I stood right there along Dad's fence, between the barn and the field. I went back in the barn, and finally I see the coyote going. I didn't know why they had him when I was looking up there. Boy, old Buster, I heard him, and here he comes. Man, the other dog's moving.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Boy, where are they coming?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: And he comes right down that damn fence, and right straight from me, I stood there, and I decided that that son of a gun, I guess, was 10 or 15 seconds before I pulled the trigger. And down he went. I think he came down over there, and he came down there. By golly, I got him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You did get him, yes?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I said, I got him. Right here he is. No, he was flying. Boy, that son of a gun was a -coming. I got about 10 seconds to come up on that hill, I said, and like that. He comes down, and bang. He was about 30 feet from me when I pulled the trigger. I used to be a fairly good shot with a rifle. I'd go to these sales they had around, and have the old come home with seconds or something. Yes, I had one old boy that was hard to beat. I had to get down and shoot pretty good to beat him. I let him beat me a couple of times. His name was Clausen, but he's not here. He went to Montana. And the boys, they couldn't shoot. They had three of them, and they'd always come up there to shoot, but they couldn't shoot. And he had an old muzzleloader, and damn thing, it shot a bullet as big as my finger, my thumb. And you load that old thing up with that bullet, and you ride down, and they got a bang with him. And they'd have to always go in and make it so he was close to us. But I beat him several times. I was right in, right at the edge of the target.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they use for a target?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Oh, they had papers all about this big square. And we had a box out there, and we had to shoot there for three or four years. And that was about six feet of dirt, you know. So, he shot at the target, and the bullet never goes through it. And it was all made out of tuba pores in the front and the back of the bullet. So, they just shot a second bullet, and they hit this dirt.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: So, sometimes he beat you, and sometimes you beat him.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I remember one time he beat me twice, and I was shooting. And he came and beat me. And one person said, you can't beat us. I said, I have beat him.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: But I said, when I got right down to business on this, why the hell, I said, he's not going to run away with it for nothing. He's got to shoot. And I said, well, I quit. I go back and sit down and let him shoot. He'd take home four or five of his, and chicken, and a bunch of geese there one time. I was down shooting. Eight of us shot. You have two bits apiece to shoot. And I walked home with four of them geese. And he said, I'll be damned if I know how in the hell you shoot to get in there like that. I see him measure it out,

speaker: Archie Clark
words: and set it on there, on the center, and measure it back. I said, damn it, if you wasn't right in there. And your boys went through them miles in the paper.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: His name was on the top of the cross. And by golly, he lived about a mile from us. I went over there one day, and I had my rifle, and I rode a kite. I had a kite, and I was going to hunt. This place, oh, it's my stand. I was up there one time, and I killed a coyote. And all the dogs, I had my two dogs. I never turned them loose. I didn't turn them loose after the scouting. Jack Burton, he had eight hounds. And he had his all in.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: There were sixteen hounds, and the two men had sixteen hounds. And another fella came out, and he was over from Rock Creek over on the other side of the potlice. On this side of the potlice, I mean. He brought three over, and he turned them in. Well, twenty -one hounds was running, and I didn't turn mine loose. And there was nothing without mine. And boy,

speaker: Archie Clark
words: then they turned them over every damn while. Well, you know, you couldn't hear yourself think.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: On that road down there with the Catholics, I was just lying with people. You had to be careful about shooting, too. And when you're shooting, I got here, you know, I took them way up on the hill, and I had mine tied up, and there was a line and all, and I made them made themselves every time. I said, I said, I said, I said, I said, and they had us up a little bit, and then finally got them set up, and then the crazy old bus would go, whoo! Now I told you to set up, I want you to keep still. He'd come down there pretty soon. Well, you know, it was about an hour's chase up on the mountain around there, and then finally he come down. He wanted to go back over towards Voss's place, over towards Zimmerman's, and over in there, that's where his heads were. He come down on this old standing line, and I was waiting.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: He's at about 100 yards of field right here, and he jumped up to that, and boy, what is he pulling?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: It was about an inch of snow on, and man, was he coming. Yes, he was a -coming. I said, well, them all them damn hounds behind me, man, I was hung out there for miles. He didn't know he was half past them, and my hound was still there. But he was in the started box, and he said, you shut up! I said, shut up, man. I got over against the hound, and I was waiting for him. I had a 30 -30 wind church, that's what I shoot. And by golly, he come down there, keep sight on him. He come through that fence, and he had to jump. Oh, there must have been about that much snow, and the wires were about this high from the ground. And he jumped through this fence, and just as he jumped through it, I didn't even have it. Boy, I got him right there, hanging right in the wire.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: John Benson is up the road, about 200 yards up that old road, he goes by the main hall. I don't know where one road turns west and one goes up on the hill. Oh, he was right there.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: He said, come down, he said, why didn't you let the hounds catch him?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: They was gaining on him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they call him?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Had a hell of a time getting him out of the fence, I was over from that fence. Ah, they had him dragged over there about 30 feet from the fence, a whole bunch of them. It was a damn thick there, a wild fat little car that they was walking on tight of his back. He got up there, trying to get down to him. John, he went over there, and he said, oh hell, that's another little damn old Peeta. He said, that'll carry him over to Peeta's, all that little damn Peeta. I'll get something of him. Well, all I got of him, my hound was right here. I thought you had your hounds in there. I don't know. I never had them in there at all. I tied them up right here, and I left them right there. Well, you damned old custody, said you. I said, I knew they were catching around. I knew where he was coming when I get down here. I said, they made one circle up on the hill on Old Street, John's place, and back over the mountain, and he come down this other ridge. As soon as he started down that ridge, I said, I knew exactly where he was going. He was coming right down here through me. And by the way, he come out there, broke into my hound's field, my hound's place, and he come right straight out, just over the ridge down there to the road. And he just come down over that, and went down in this, come right down there, and he jumped through the fence out of them hounds. Man, oh man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had a favorite stand? You had your own stand? You used to take a lot? One place?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I killed about 20 coyotes there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you pick it? How did you decide that was a good place?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Well, I was the other hound running through there, said, damn, I see. I came down there. Little slow on them, damn boy. I said, I tell you just where I went, I was standing by them, went right through there. I bet you missed that stand of mine 10 feet. Oh, when he used to say, well, he wasn't there this time, Archie. He didn't get down there. No, I said, I didn't make it. I didn't want to make it. My dog was running, I said. There was a lead too, boy. Old Buster, I said, and Judy was both in the lead. He had a pair of jipping dick, they called them. And they were right behind him. My dog just got in the lead somewhere, both of them. Old Buster was pressing that coyote right down through there. Nobody there. He went up the road onto another 10 of them in there. And I shot one just about two hours before this happened. I was up there on the hill. I shot him. I dragged the coyote down. We had Jap, Burns, dogs in, and Nathan, my own. Jap had a pride out that morning. They take two of them, but they left them home. Anyway, I had pride. I had about nine dogs after him. I was making plenty of racket. Well, I got in the way, come down there, and I got him. I went over there and I hounded. I got there just to hound him. I let someone run down the road about 10 feet before they stopped. I went and got him in the tail, and I dragged him down. I went right on top of him. I then fired him a little bit, and then fired him down to the barn. Told him in the barn there.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Old dog just left. They just pulled right back up there somewhere. The boys were the kind of neighborhood. They didn't know where the boys were. Jap says, I was the first coyote. He took me right up here. He just came through. He told me afterwards. I caught two of them dogs and took them up there and turned them loose. Here come the whole dam works. I turned right along. I had to get behind the trees and out of the road. The dam was a -coming. Old Charlie had a great greyhound. Damned he wasn't in the mud. He was running too. Boy, they took that down. They made a circle around. They came around towards the gym room. Then they came back about an hour or so. Just about on that image right there. He crossed the road. Went around too. Went on the way to the mountain. He made one circle around the mountain. Then he came back down. He came right to me. It was the old dam. He came down and he drove right through here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was the stand? Where was that stand at? What kind of spot was it? You know what, the Mountain Home Grange?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Yeah. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That used to be your stand?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Well,

speaker: Archie Clark
words: just a little bit south of that. Just a little bit south. There was four or five big trees. I'm down about this little, hanging down. I get in there, face myself, and a big old log in. I sit on that. I see him come across the flat and I raise myself up. Cock -a -mole gun. Get it ready. And I never missed one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did they all go through there? Why do you think they all went through that spot?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Well, that was their runway. You could go up there and see tracks across. Then farmers out there, there was a cow or a calf or a cow or a horse or something. They'd be overeating on it. Then they'd go back, come down through there. They'd always come in the night though. Lots of hounds after them. Then they'd run these times. Oh boy, I don't think Bernice. They made about one circle up there on the hill and they'd come down. Until they come to me, they crossed up on the hill and an old log roadie went down and he crossed there. I heard the rifle go quack, you know. And it wasn't just about five minutes. I heard Jack say, you son of a bitch, don't eat him up. Don't eat him up, you son of a bitch. Oh hell, I did that job where he got my cow. He's up there ahead of me, just up here on the hill. And just over the hill, about 100 yards, oh what's his name, had an orchard up there. Had an orchard up there and he'd, all his food and stuff would land around in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd go for that, would they?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Yes, they'll eat that damn stuff. And that's where this cow is going, he's going to cross through the Japs and he's dragging me, turn him loose at him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm going a little hard, but what do you got to say with him?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Oh damn you, I said that cow is coming right to me. Yes he was, he's coming right back down this road. He get on the road right up there and he stopped a little bit and then finally he started to smelt down on the ground and he looked at the net and he'd smelt down to me. Had him have it. One of them said, I'm glad you got him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think they were pretty smart, the coyotes?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I used to have one team. Mother come out the damn door one time and she'd come out and, ah it's easy how it. And he'd land right there, well right about there. And I'd been petting him like that and I had my hand laying down there like that. And I hit him off. And some of it he bit me through there, he bit that finger out, oh and several scratches all over. And I pulled loose. And I did it, I just said that he quit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said what do you want?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: He said that damn coyote bit me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said why did you sleep?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Yes I did. He woke up and he grabbed me.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Well I don't know, I had him tame and I was loose. And I put him in his pen. And as soon as I, I was the only one that he'd have anything to do with it. I don't know how am I. I put him in my lap and take him around and said I'm going to rub him. Just let somebody else rub him. Just that quick. I don't know how in the hell he could tell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you raise him from a pup?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you find him?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Found a nest. He came out of his nest and he came right up like that. He stood right there and looked at him. And I raised him and grabbed him by the back of the neck. And I said he was about ten years, eleven years old. He had a sack tied up. I put that in by himself, he was a team. And put him in that sack, tied it up. We had five in that other sack. All in there together. We got one more out there. That's all we could find. Oh I used to get them damn cows in the hole and I'd go down to the fence and cut a piece of wire about five or six feet long. This ended on a stick.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I'd work that wire down in there until I hit him. I started to pull him out. And I had my cow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you do with him then? Them cowdies?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Yeah the ones you got that way. I'd take them down to the house and put them in a little pen I had there. And I got them little ones I didn't get very much. I got a dollar a piece for the hides and three dollars money.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I had seven and Lincoln had eight. All the big cowdies and we'd send them off together. Both of us did. And I got three dollars more for my cow than Lincoln for his. For me and his.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where'd you send him to?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Oh gee, I can remember. He had the money.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: That's where he stayed. My God, you know. I got the money. Three dollars a piece for all of them. I had seven, he had eight. That's fifteen cows all together. Just the hides.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he, what did he do?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I forget now. He took something off. He couldn't be sold again.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Oh that was, yeah. We'd take and kill them. And they was all alive. And that's when I said it was a tame and I had him home. And that summer, by all you know, he'd sit there and look at me. Take the team out and go up in the field. Sit there and look at me and look like that and be on that team. One day I said, mother I said will you come out here and watch my horse in a minute. I'm going to take him with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are you doing?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I said I'm plowing on that black field over there. And he can step on my lap. And by golly I know I got him up on that plow and I started up and the plow was kind of jumping around. And he went up and looked at me and said, finally look up and lick me on the chin.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Finally he got over that. He didn't jump at all. He just turned around by that and laid right down in my lap. Oh he isn't that long and he isn't about that high. He's about that high.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a pup yet?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Up that back of my house, up there in that old house. Up there in that there's a bunch of timber up there. Mother says, you know Archie, there's something that catches my chickens in the morning. There is, yeah. Well I said I'll tell you I'm going to get up and take the rifle out here about four o 'clock. And then I let a bunch of brush right up there and a little bit of stuff. I'm going to get right behind that and squat down right there. The wind is blowing from the east a little bit, not much I said. And by golly I can get her. She got a nest up there someplace and I never went up to look for it but I know it's up there. And by golly I know she came out of that bush down there and just stopped and looked all around. She went to smell it all on the ground and finally she came over to the fence and she came right up to the fence. Right up to the same place where I tell you I was sitting up there and she came over and shot him. And I was up on the hill, just a little bunch of brush there. And she came right up there and I came to his bus. 30 feet from me when I pulled the trigger. I got right in there and she was just walking and trying to walk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that your pet, Caio?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: That was another time. I had my pet up the house then.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I killed him.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I had to go to pot lights and he got to be a dog.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: And I got up there at 22 one day and he was looking out that other way through the fence. And I let him have him right in the back of the head. And boy, he heard the racket I guess and that's all there was to it. And I walked over there and got him and took him down to the green room. I skinned him out, saved him. I tell him to pull him down, pull him down, no way to cut the legs off. Right in here is the joint there. Left him on the height. I don't know whether I got any more out of him or not, but I had five of them. I think he had four and we sent them off together. I got a three dollar bounty. And they were paying seven dollars for a while, but that wasn't then. They had come down a little bit. I think I got five dollars a piece for them. But it was hard to see them down like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think you could train a coyote to be like a pet dog?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Oh no, no. It was a damn fool to hell that young coyote had it. I don't think it changed anything. He'd forget it. And after the cows went right there, I called him and I said, well you'll have to go get him I guess. And he looked at me and had his tail up.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: He looked at me and he started hitting me on the foot. My guy went and I let him go. He was about ten feet ahead of me. I was walking behind him and he said, you don't know where to get them cows. I had three milk cows I had to get out of that bunch.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I went down and got these two and I started them up and I had to go back and get a black one. I said, well it's all black.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: That little devil was barking at me trying to get her to come. I got out of my steer.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: He said, the devil?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: He knows the cows. He knew where the cows were. He was barking at me.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I went down and he said, he won't move for you will you boy. He couldn't get him to waggle his tail or nothing. He just stood up and he had me hanging down like that. He looked at me and I went around the cow and I started out and got her going over the other two. We took him on in. That's the only time I ever seen him go into the herd. He was down there barking at the cow. He was barking right at his head. His head just kept getting behind him. He went, bow bow, bow bow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well did you kill him mostly for the money or did you kill him for the sport?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Sport. I went all cut in the fall and did everything in the jumps. I took my wife there one day. She walked pretty good. She put on a pair of pants.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: She put them on and she showed her coat down and put the spinner up and I had to get them up for her. She went with me. She said she was warm all the time. I took her over to this stand where I had to get them. By the way, Lincoln and I both went. We got over there and there was another boy.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: He lived just up over the hill. He just went down below his house where they come through.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: He said, did you come up there? I said, did you hear that shooting?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I thought I heard somebody shooting. That was me. I shot three shots at that son of a bitch and never touched him. He didn't have the arms sighted on him. He had the arms sighted on him to kill him. By golly, he said they were hard to kill. In the way, the dogs were chasing them. They were after that same bunch. There were three in the bunch, but they stayed together. When they got up on the hill, two of them broke off to the right up there. The other one came down and they kept coming. They were all after him. He came in the night before and he was going to cut around the cottage with them that day. They got into that house and bore them. I got two more of them, six of them. They kept running. Six of them came down over that hill. That car was running too. I said, he's coming right here. I was waiting for him to hit the road. I didn't have it. He never got out of it. He said, you killed him.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I said, what did you do on his matter?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I said, all I shot for was kill him.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I said, good shot, wasn't it?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Well, yes, I don't know.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I went over and got him. I got my two and tied him up. He was coming down.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I said, well, I'll tie up his two dogs too. I don't know who these others are. They got in up there someplace.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: He came down and I can't think of his name.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I was the second on the piano. He had a letter up to Niebkirk. I know who you mean, but I can't think of his name either. I've heard about him. Good fiddling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Fiddled?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Man, damn he get fiddled. He had another man named John Denlep. He was a Missourian. Boy, he get fiddled.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Then they had C4 more than one of them. I had to take it with the whole words, all of them. I had to take it on the organ.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I never played hard enough to hear the violin all the time. That one made it nice too, up there in front. They clapped their hands and hollered, give us another one. I wasn't fiddling. I said, I'll try this and see, D or whatever you want.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I don't want to see a little bit. I said, oh, let her go. And he looked and I was feeling like a little bit. We had you do stands all the time up there. As in the old Niebkirk schoolhouse. We were full of people.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Maybe for dances. They didn't dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much drinking at those dances?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: No. All we had was a shirt tail dance. There was lots of them. We had a hall down there where I lived. We had dances there three winters. And about every other Saturday. And they had literally every night up there in the schoolhouse. People would always be jammed and full of people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they come from Potlatch to go out there?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: No, mostly all the campers. Down to Farmington, Garfield, come from Puse. Not very many. They always had something to do in Potlatch. Same thing.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: They always had plenty of fiddlers. I don't know if I'll let this all in you. It's fiddle. Old Missouri. I don't want to. I used to second form all the time. I used to second the whole bus.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Eddie Biciger. He used to second too. Charlie Biciger, he used to second. That's his brother. He's dead now. I guess there's only one left.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Anyway, I'd say, Charles, come up here. Take this thing a little bit. I'd have to go take a leak or something. He said, okay, and he sat down. And they didn't like him. People didn't like him. He played a little too hard. He couldn't hear the violin very much. All played a little bit lighter.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Makes a lot of difference.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Oh, they just had them old congealed bands. Talk about playing. They don't hit them. The whole, like that,

speaker: Archie Clark
words: they're pretty good at it too. They used to have some good times. Always had good times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the woman doing?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She's sitting down and stuff?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Just like I am with you. I was a pretty well acquainted man all over the country. I always went and I was going. Charlie Crummell down there lived there on the flat. Owned that big flat down there going to Garfield. He had eight hounds. He had two and he never took out. They were males. Females, I mean. He never took them out.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I'll have to hear one time. I was sick. I tried to tell Lake what I'd seen with that dog.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, did he knock him down?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Hell, I said, he went about four feet and landed about six foot of snow. And so, I said, I was right behind him about ten feet. I walked over there and got him out of the air and pulled him out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, what son of a bitch did you get up?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I'll kill him. I said, I'll tell him when I get home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, what?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: I'll tell him when I get home.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Old big stag hound was. He was a great big hound.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the hound do?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: They came out on this trail where old Charlie Crummell was. And on the trail up above they had taken that. It was only about thirty feet apart. That's where he went into this trail. He must have seen Charlie and took up there. They came right down through this trail and they went over Charlie. He was going to go over there and he just knocked old Charlie about. He went from here to that great big pile of snow. I laughed. I got straightened up and I turned around and I said, I got him by the hand and I pulled him out of there. I said, Charlie, Charlie Crummell, if I had a code I got to stop taking your picture. He said, but see, I'll let him know. I said, I don't know, but I'll shoot him as soon as he gets home. I said, don't shoot the dog. Hell, he was right up there the rest of the time. He wasn't barking but he was right with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come all you had was two dogs and these other guys had more? How come you only had two hounds and some of these guys had five and six? Two is enough for you?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Yeah, I got them too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I remember one time, you never seen that old place, did you? Where was it? You ever go down that road around that road and then go back into the big area?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Yeah, is that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who still lives out there?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: The highway and come up here. The next house right on the corner. You drive up the hill and down around and Walt Kitteler used to live right in the next house. And then Ellis Waddell, they're all widows, they're all gone. Walt lost his wife and Ellis lost her man. And you go down the road there and there's another road that breaks back in that other old place. It's about 200 yards back in there. There's nothing. Everything is torn out, all gone. And you go down a little further and there's another house. That's where my brother lives, at Verne Clark. He's dead now. He died here. He's dead now. Once and a half ago, I guess. Anyway, that's where my brother lives. He goes on down to the corner down there. He goes on down to the corner and starts turning there. I used to live in that house there. I lived there two years in that place. Had my hounds over there and they'd call me up. The damn cows were howling here last night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, are they coming over or what are they now?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: They said I don't know where they are now, but they were sure howling here last night. That's where them both farmers are in there. I think I was caught up in the lake and about the other way. And then there would be eight or ten of them farmers come. Old George Benson, he lived up on the hill there and he had nine hounds.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: He got his feet to feed them. He'd hear about an old cow or a horse or something and he'd go and cut the damn thing in two. And tied it around the hind legs and he'd drag it up towards the end of his place, feed it in the place. Down below the house. I'd take the dogs down there and they'd eat on it and then all the rest of them would go down there. We all soon had a pretty well cleaned up. Oh, boss, you lost one. You had an old barn there up on the hill. Oh well.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: When you come around that bend down there, you know where the breaking is. Well, on the highway. I mean, stay on the highway and they'd come around and they'd come up a little hill there. And the cattle would shoot through there. The cattle used to go back and forth. The boss would have to go back and forth. Anyway, they'd get down to the creek and get a drink. They had a fence around there so they'd have to go back up in there. By golly, you know, people, they'd watch. If they'd seen me come across the hill, I'd sit on the wall. All the Catholics would pray and watch. I had dogs. The damn dog would get behind the tree and stop him. But I kept him getting out of it and I just cleaned it out in the timber on the field. I had geese all over the damn country. I'd go across and they'd watch. And then they'd phone each other and tell them where I was going. And then the pilot, hell, I'd get a bunch of other pilots.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: One day, I was sitting right in this old stand I had there. I stayed in that old stand for years. I don't know, I killed a lot of cows there. I killed three cows there one day. Three of them. And I'm all having an old barn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think he came over and he said, You gonna skin them out tonight, Art?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: No. I said, I think I'll tie them on Bill. I said, I don't like Billy. And I didn't know how I trained that dog. I didn't know how I trained this damn horse. I'd take him up there and throw the reins up over a limb. He wouldn't pull them off. He'd hear me holler. And I'd be maybe a quarter mile from him. And I'd holler at him. And I'd say, Hey, Bill! And he'd come to me. I'd holler at him. I waited about two minutes and he'd nicker. He's got me. He knows where I'm at. He'd come to me. Yes. And all the hell I trained that boy, I tell you, it was hard to say. And he'd come up through there and come out through there and the reins would sort of snap together and I'd hang over his neck. And I'd take him up over a limb or something. He never pulled them off. And now they'd be right there when I'd come back down to him. All he had to do was turn his head like that and he'd pull them off. He'd almost be there waiting for me. I called him a quarter of a mile and he'd come right up to me. Hey, Bill! I'd yell. And the person would say, Whoopee! Bill! I was like that. He'd come. And the person would hear him nicker. And as soon as I heard him nicker, I knew he heard me. He's coming. He's coming to me. Now how I trained that time of the day, I trained him.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Oh, well, I'll tell you how I did that. He'd go out in the field and I'd always have a pen. There was some feet in it. And I'd go, Hey, Bill! And the person would come up on the hill and stop there and look at me. Hey, Bill! Come on! And I'd come up and give me feet. Take it or this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The only one to do it?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: And that's the way I got him in the brush. Damn, I worked at that old satellite around two places. He'd come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they'd say, How in the world did you train that damn horse to come to you?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Well, I'd tell you, I'd get him to get short. I used to go out there on the hill above the house. And I'd holler, Bill! And I'd say, Almost half mile from me. Pretty soon I'd see him walking out and looking, looking, looking. And I'd say, Hey, Bill! Come on! And that's all I had to say. He was coming. He felt good. He'd kick up his heels a little bit too and here he's coming. Boy, he'd just come up there just to see him. He'd come right up to me. Sometimes the other horse would fire him in too. And I'd give him his feet. They'd all be around me. I'd give him a little bit of this and a little bit of that and all of them. And he wanted that feet himself. He'd walk right around me if I used to get it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I can see how you got him to come now, but how'd you get him to stay? How'd you get him to stay? Stayed? There? How'd you get him to stay with his reins thrown over a tree?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Oh, I just left him there.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: And then the other one there too, you know. I think that the other guy was there too. He was thrown and had a white face and part of the tail was white. He said, Oh, Bill, never go bother her. And she never come around Bill. He said, Oh, just stand there. Look at you. And he'd stand there. I'll throw a blanket over his coat. So he'd stay warm. He'd go around his neck, buckle it there, and had a hole up here to let the blanket down through there. I let the blanket down through the horn so he couldn't move it.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Once you get done riding, get everything you want.

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Tipperary Falls, Minnesota, way up in front of Minnesota.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the trip out like? What was the trip out like? What did we have? No, what was it like?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Sipping out here. We had an old boxcar, Milwaukee boxcar, and we filled it up. Horses, cows, chickens, and all our bedding and everything like that from the house. And we crawled in there. And we were 11 days down the road coming through River Falls, Minnesota, you'll hear the blues. I got off the routine that morning and I took the horses all out. We tore down the manger and everything, took the horses all out. And then we took the cows out first, and then we took the horses out. There's just a little pitch off here. And I lay them down. I fed them hay. We didn't have much hay left, so we bought out. And I fed the cows. And we left the stuff in and tore the mangers all out and everything out of there. We got everything out. And Dad says we'll have to get that harness down. We had it hanging up over a bunch of chickens and stuff in there. And we had one colt with us, he was a year old. By all you know. Yes, Steve was a year old almost. No, Steve was past a year old. We came out here in the fall. And Steve was past a year old. We brought her with us. Steve was our pet. I'm going to talk about our pet. If you'd seen me in that colt, you'd have laughed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You'd have said, how in the hell did you ever train her?

speaker: Archie Clark
words: Well, I trained her. I didn't have nothing else to do. I trained her. I said, come on now, get around here. I trained her. I stopped the old hilt and she turned around and she'd kick up and heel that mince. She turned around and she'd say, come on, get out of here. And I'd say, around you go, around and around and around. Right in front of the house. And they'd all come up because the wind was looking out. And around and around she'd go down. I'd say, whoop, come on, come here. She stopped and we trotted right up to her. I stopped and I patted her head. Ever just sitting right. Now I said, come on, let's go the other way. I leaned a little bit and I said, come on, get out, get out. And I had a black snake, a whip. And I snapped that and boy, she turned around and kicked up at me. And boy, she'd go around and around and around. And she made four or five circles around. And I said, okay, come on in. And she came right up to me. Well, she wasn't a year old when I started that honor. I had a rope on and I needed it. Here's a rope, I had to handle the rope. And I pulled her in. She'd work on one and she'd just, oh, take her to the death. And then finally I got turned loose. And the first time she went to the boy and all of it, she said, what the hell, you just had to go to the boy. And she would go to the boy and I would go down and get her and lead her back out. Put the rope on her, everything was fine. I had a hook like that. There was no place to pass it here. I had that hooked in the holler. And I got her going around and around and around. Finally I get that, I get around and hook it and drop it down. And she'd just keep on going, going around and around and around. And I'd go, whoop, come on, that's good enough, come in here. And she'd come in to me. I hooked that on and everything was fine. I was tied to the other way.
